species: Humpback Whale + Orca -- same-channel simultaneous vocalization
sound_type: Multi-species acoustic overlap: Humpback whale song (Megaptera novaeangliae, 20-2000 Hz primary, 10-30 minute culturally-transmitted song sequence) simultaneously active with Southern Resident Killer Whale social calls (Orcinus orca, 100-20,000 Hz, 100+ discrete call types, pod-specific dialects) in the same acoustic channel in the San Juan Islands, Salish Sea, summer. The overlap zone: 100-500 Hz where both species' primary vocal energy is present. FIRST MULTI-SPECIES VOCALIZATION ENTRY IN ENGINE.
ecosystem: San Juan Islands, Salish Sea, inland waters -- the labyrinthine archipelago of the San Juan Island chain provides acoustic concentration and propagation for both species simultaneously. Humpback feeding grounds (herring, sand lance, krill) overlap geographically with SRKW Chinook salmon territory during summer. Both species simultaneously resident during June-September.
season: Summer (June-September, peak July-August for simultaneous presence)
conservation_status: Humpback: Least Concern (IUCN, North Pacific population recovering since 1966 whaling moratorium). SRKW: Endangered (ESA); ~73 animals as of 2024; primary threat: prey depletion (Chinook salmon decline), vessel noise, contaminants.
milestone: FIRST MULTI-SPECIES VOCALIZATION ENTRY. E=8. Two species with independently culturally transmitted vocalizations simultaneously active in the same acoustic space. Humpback song: the most complex song sequence in the animal kingdom, culturally transmitted at 1,500 km/year (Noad et al. 1999). SRKW dialects: pod-specific call repertoires transmitted through social learning. The Salish Sea contains the full Humpback-SRKW acoustic ecology simultaneously.

coordinates.description: San Juan Islands archipelago, Washington State. Humpback primary feeding areas: Admiralty Inlet, Rosario Strait, San Juan Channel, Haro Strait. SRKW primary summer range: Haro Strait, San Juan Channel, Boundary Pass. Maximum overlap: Haro Strait between San Juan Island and Vancouver Island.

acoustic_data.humpback_profile: Song sequence: 10-30 minutes, repeated continuously for hours; male singers produce song during both breeding grounds (winter) and feeding grounds (summer, less frequent). Frequency: primary elements 30-500 Hz, full range 20-2,000 Hz, infrasonic components (20-30 Hz) for long-range propagation. Song structure: hierarchical -- units -> phrases -> themes -> song; the hierarchical structure enables cultural variation at multiple levels simultaneously.
acoustic_data.orca_profile: SRKW social calls: discrete calls (J pod: ~30 identified types, 1-20 kHz), whistles (1-18 kHz), burst-pulse sounds (100 Hz-20 kHz), echolocation clicks (20-100 kHz). Call rates during active socializing: 1-5 calls per minute per animal; pod vocalizing together produces dense acoustic output. E=9 at greeting ceremony (degree 311); E=8 in normal social context during Humpback overlap.
acoustic_data.overlap_zone: 100-500 Hz: Humpback moans and low cries + SRKW low-frequency social calls and burst-pulse components. Research (Parks et al., Fournet et al.) documents behavioral modification in both species during simultaneous vocalization: some studies show SRKW reduce low-frequency call rates; others document apparent turn-taking. Acoustic interference in the overlap zone is a research frontier.
acoustic_data.e8_calibration: E=8: (1) combined acoustic output of two of the ocean's most vocal species, both at significant individual energy levels; (2) Humpback song complexity is the animal-kingdom maximum -- the most elaborate sustained vocal sequence produced by any non-human organism; (3) SRKW in non-ceremony context (E=9 at greeting ceremony) reduced to E=8 in normal social vocalization; (4) cultural transmission on both tracks simultaneously is the engine's richest acoustic-cultural event; (5) conservation significance: both species are recovery stories in the Salish Sea, and their simultaneous acoustic presence represents ecological restoration.

nodes.title: First Multi-Species Vocalization Entry -- Two Cultural Traditions in One Channel
nodes.description: The Humpback-Orca same-channel event is the engine's first multi-species acoustic entry. Both species have culturally transmitted vocalizations: Humpback song evolves at 1,500 km/year through social learning; SRKW pod dialects are family-specific and transmitted generationally. Two independently evolving cultural traditions in one acoustic space.

nodes.title: Humpback Song as Cultural Transmission -- The 1,500 km/year Spread
nodes.description: Noad et al. 1999: new song type originated in eastern Australian Humpback population, spread westward across Pacific, replaced existing song type across thousands of km within a few breeding seasons. Rate: approximately 1,500 km/year. Mechanism: social learning. The song the engine documents in the San Juans is part of a Pacific-wide culturally evolving acoustic tradition.

nodes.title: SRKW Pod Dialects -- Cultural Transmission in Orca
nodes.description: SRKW J, K, and L pod dialects are distinct from each other and from transient and offshore Orca populations. The call repertoires are transmitted through social learning across generations. Degree 311 documented the SRKW greeting ceremony (E=9). Degree 317 documents SRKW in multi-species acoustic context (E=8).

nodes.title: Humpback Recovery -- The Conservation Success Context
nodes.description: North Pacific Humpback nearly eliminated by commercial whaling (pre-1966). Recovery since IWC moratorium. Salish Sea Humpback sightings increasing over past two decades: from rare to regular summer individuals. The simultaneous Humpback-SRKW acoustic presence represents ecological restoration -- a species that was acoustically absent from this ecosystem for decades is back.

cross_references.relationship: E=9 (greeting ceremony maximum) vs. E=8 (normal social context during Humpback overlap). Same SRKW population, different behavioral context.

E=8 + E=7 = E=15. CONVERGENCE PAIRING: two bands in one studio (Temple) vs. two species in one channel (Humpback-Orca). Cultural transmission on both tracks: human recorded music + whale/orca song evolution.
Full Humpback song sequence as solo acoustic entry: complete 30-minute song, cultural evolution documentation, male singer on San Juan feeding grounds. Future entry preserves the full species solo.
